message:
This is an interesting essay, but I'm not sure the distinction the author draws between the ultimate decision of the "imperial power of the state", the assessment of future historians and how a story is told is that compelling.

Generally there are different ways to portray many conflicts, depending on your point of view. [A recent New York Times piece](http://www.nytimes.com/2016/02/25/upshot/measuring-donald-trumps-supporters-for-intolerance.html?smid=tw-share&amp;_r=1) claimed that 38 percent of Donald Trump supporters wish that the South had won the US Civil War. 

It would be fair to assume that they do not see the Confederacy as the bad guys. I'm sure they could even point to the overwheening power of the union crushing a way of life and other reasons as justification for this distinction. In other words, there are many ways to tell a given story, depending on emphasis. And that is how history is written.

message:
I think it's possible that you are taking the saying too literally as a tactical observation, while in reality it serves as an exclamation of moral objectivism, of a belief that *there is such a thing as justice*, and therefore we are moving towards it.

Sure, if you look at a fight for social justice on a level of personal events, you might see injustices happen. You might even see failed policies, lost battles, rolled back laws. 

But the point of the saying is not that liberals will always keep winning everything all the time, it's that there is such a thing as right and wrong, the world is not randomly swinging back and forth.

That even if injustice exists, it is defined as the temporary lack of of justice, not as the natural state of the world. 

There was a blog post about that matter, although it was addressed at the phrase "they are on the wrong side of history", it applies to this one as well. i'm going to quote a bit, but you should [read the whole thing](http://www.patheos.com/blogs/slacktivist/2015/07/02/try-not-to-be-the-bad-guy-in-the-story-what-it-means-to-be-on-the-wrong-side-of-history/).  


> I think the confusion here comes from the use of the word “history,” which these days connotes an academic discipline more than Clio’s art of story-telling. “The wrong side of history” isn’t really about that academic discipline — about the assessment of future historians. It’s about stories.

> “The wrong side of history” is not a claim about the future, but a claim about the present — about what’s happening right now. And it’s not a claim about winners or losers or about the inevitable progress of the dialectic. It’s about good guys and bad guys.

> Every rhetorical invocation of “The wrong side of history” is an invitation to ask Mitchell and Webb’s question: “[Are we the baddies?](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=ToKcmnrE5oY)” (Or, if you want a more highbrow formulation, we can paraphrase Dickens: “Shall we turn out to be the heroes of our own story?”)

> The sheriff of Hadleyville was the good guy. The sheriff of Nottingham was the bad guy. Neither of those (probably) is a certifiably “historic” figure, but it still makes sense to say that former was on the right side of history while the latter was on the wrong side of it. Yes, these are “only” stories — not real history. But stories matter.

> The stories of the sheriff of Hadleyville and the sheriff of Nottingham inform how we evaluate the real-life story of the sheriff of Birmingham. Bull Connor was on the wrong side of history.

> That is obvious now, and it was obvious then. He was the baddie. We don’t say this because the imperial power of the state ultimately sided against him. We don’t say this because of some arrogant claim of inevitable, dialectical progress, or even because of some humbler assertion of eschatological faith in the long-bending arc of the moral universe. We say this because there’s no other way to tell this story.

message:
It will be *effectively* zero. Let’s say that a car costs $10,000 to manufacture currently, plus $1,000 for transportation, and it’s sold for $20,000. $2,000 of that is materials, and the rest is labor (I’m pulling this number out of my ass, so correct me if I’m wrong) whichever company gets full automation first (assuming it will come at once, and not gradually, for the sake of argumentation), this company, in order to increase their demanded quantity will decrease their prices, and then another company will want to have buyers too, so they will automate and decrease their prices as well, then another company will also automate eventually, and decrease their prices. The beauty of natural competition in a capitalistic society.

That will cause the car’s final price, that used to be $17,000, to be, say, $6,000 ($2,000 for costs, $1,000 for transportation, etc; also, dealerships exist, and they will want their profit, so there’s that). OPs example was bad because he used a product that has essentially no significant price ‘wiggle room’, plus *nothing* will ever be free. So you’re right, companies won’t give up their profit, they will just drop prices. And collusion is illegal, I believe that will be a BIG problem once this happens, and it’s virtually the only thing that will possibly keep prices up.

Along with that, probably comes some deflation as jobs are lost, hence less money to the public, and that’s when universal basic income would kick in. So we can expect something similar to what I described to happen, but never for things to just be free, because that wouldn’t even pay for the transportation of the goods.

I hope I answered your (sarcastic) comment properly, if you have any questions you can ask away and I’ll try to defend my point.

message:
I think the problem people have with cultural appropriation is that it you can easily misrepresent the culture you're borrowing from in a way that perpetuates a stereotype that puts them at a greater cultural distance from "your culture" than they actually are.

Suppose all Germans represented in your media always wear Lederhosen, have a beer in their hand, and speak in yodels. These are all distinctly (southern) German tropes, none have an inherent negative connotation, and you could just be using them to signal German-ness to the audience. At some point this becomes harmful, if people start to associate Germans with these, and view them as more foreign than they really are.

People do get over-sensitive about it at times, but note that most people would only take offense in cultural appropriation that links back to their people - I doubt many Indians will resent you for liking chicken tikka, because that doesn't link you back to the people of India, while some might be offended by you wearing a sari, because that's perceived by others in a way that links directly back to the Indian people, and appears foreign in the West.

This is especially true if you associate with other properties stereotypical to these people that they don't necessarily want to associate with themselves as a people, for example if you wear Native American clothes and view yourself as "having a connection with the earth", or if you adopt a faux-AAVE accent and view yourself as "gangsta", etc.

EDIT: There are too many comments in this spirit to respond individually - I'm not expressing personal moral judgment on whether any particular type of cultural appropriation is good or bad, and I'm not personally offended by any of it myself. I'm only trying to explore what logic may drive people who *are* offended by appropriation of their culture, even if I personally tend to agree with most of the caveats expressed in the comments, because this seems to be a common sentiment even among some people who are otherwise very rational.

It's all about context and respect. When someone borrows elements from another culture, it should be done thoughtfully, acknowledging the origins and meanings behind those elements. It's not about being overly sensitive; it's about mutual respect and understanding.

message:
>That's not a flaw, that's part of the party. Becoming the candidate for the party comes with a lot of benefits. You get full use of their money, resources, and connections. They don't want that to be abused, so they exert some control over the party. It's like the owners of a company holding a big chunk of voting shares. It's perfectly reasonable.
Bernie got screwed because he wasn't democratic. I like Bernie and I even voted for Bernie. He only ran as a democrat to use the party for election. These parties aren't resources available to anybody, they are established for a purpose.

What seperates the DNC in that case from being an organisation? A corporation if you will, that has a 50% chance to run the government? I would completely understand if there is an ecology of political parties, because in that case the american voter base can act as a selective force to put pressure towards parties that DO respond to the people's desires..

But with the DNC you're talking about an organisation that has roughly a 50% chance of getting into power, every election, almost guaranteed. See, my problem here is that where do the American people come into the democratic national party, if they're acting like a private organisation that 'samples' the public's view point but choses a candidate based on internal choices? What's the public's stake in this? 

Or more practically, what if I don't want Hillary, but would be fine with another candidate? It seems the majority of Americans think both candidates are very very poor. This seems to be a direct result of a two party system. Given that why is it even considered remotely allowable that those parties can internally choose who to prop up to represent them as President?


>Yet, the president hasn't ever used that power to influence the election. He could direct the DOJ or IRS to investigate the Trump Foundation, for example. Not even Trump has claimed that Obama is abusing executive power in favor of Hillary. If there was even a hint of that, he'd be all over it.

That's exactly what I'm saying though. There is no legislation that prevents this, at the moment it's just frowned upon and we're depending on the actual candidates to defend themselves against this.

> The system of Super-delegates seems at best out of date, and at worse completely batshit crazy. 

That's not a flaw, that's part of the party. Becoming the candidate for the party comes with a lot of benefits. You get full use of their money, resources, and connections. They don't want that to be abused, so they exert some control over the party. It's like the owners of a company holding a big chunk of voting shares. It's perfectly reasonable.

Bernie got screwed because he wasn't democratic. I like Bernie and I even voted for Bernie. He only ran as a democrat to *use the party* for election. These parties aren't resources available to anybody, they are established for a purpose.

> Money in politics.

I'm not sure this is a big issue. Jeb Bush has enormous amounts of funding and handily lost the primaries. Bernie was a good example of how candidates can still get tons of money from regular people. Hillary has spent nearly twice as much as Trump, but she doesn't have much to show for it. I'd like there to be transparency with opaque sources like super PACs, but I don't see a problem with the money existing.

>  I mean the President has all kinds of veto powers on Bills, and executive actions.

Yet, the president hasn't ever used that power to influence the election. He could direct the DOJ or IRS to investigate the Trump Foundation, for example. Not even Trump has claimed that Obama is abusing executive power in favor of Hillary. If there was even a *hint* of that, he'd be all over it.

message:
Of course there's a political context. See the notion of the "[invisible hand](https://www.reddit.com/r/dredmorbius/comments/4cyroa/adam_smiths_lost_legacy_or_why_you_should_read/)." 

If fundamentals of economic parlance like *that* have a history of obscurantism, how can you possibly imagine the rest of the field is devoid of them?

>without any empirical evidence to back up its "laws" of supply and demand.

Lol, fucking what? I clearly remember in my intro course the professor showing tables of elasticities, which essentially prove the laws of supply and demand. "Not a hard science" wtf does that even mean? Just because it's "not a hard science" doesn't mean there aren't clear relationships that are more or less certainly true

You disagree that when the price of things go up, people demand less of it?

If you think there is a conservative bias in economics, you are delusional. The democrat:republican ratio among economists is 2.5:1

There really is no "political context" for the vast majority of what you learn in economics. Only for empirical stuff is there political context as often you are dealing with the effects of laws made by politicians

@optical-ice I get where you're coming from but seriously, the basics of econ are pretty straightforward. Like, if stuff costs more, people buy less. It's not some hidden agenda. And yeah, my prof showed us all the data backing it up. Not saying there's no politics at all, but let's not throw the baby out with the bathwater here.

@distinct-notch Yeah, but isn't it funny how these "basics" always seem to justify the status quo? Coincidence? I bet Adam Smith wouldn't recognize his "invisible hand" in today’s economy. #justsaying

message: Guys, let's keep it cool. We're all here to talk about the story, right? No need to turn it into a big show. #PeaceOut 🤘
The hormones a body naturally produces in puberty permanently alter a child's biology. Why is that not abusive, if artificial puberty is?

a 15-year-old doesn't just walk into a surgery clinic and say, cut off my penis/breasts and make me a woman/man and they do it. That is not how it works.

They get counseling from multiple doctors, therapists, and other medical professionals before that ever happens. surgery is the end result of years of prescreening and treatments that must happen before it's even on the table- usually at 18 years old at the very earliest. 

You are citing an example that literally does not exist. 

The puberty stopping hormone treatments that trans kids are eligible for do exactly that, they stop puberty temporarily, and it resumes when they come off of them.

The side effects of temporarily stopping puberty for a trans kid could make the world of difference when they hit adulthood, and go through irreversible hormonal bodily changes that make them even more uncomfortable in their own skin. The risk-reward ratio makes it a moot argument.

If they were wrong about being trans, they end up going through puberty after coming off the puberty blockers and that's that. There are side effects, but the risk is being misgendered for your whole life vs going through puberty late. Anyone who's contemplating going through this situation is getting lots of advice and therapy and counseling from a multitude of doctors. 


It's not a child making the decision alone.

I understand the process involves a lot of checks and balances, but it still feels like we're tinkering with something so fundamental. I mean, I've seen how delicate nature can be, especially with birds. Once their environment changes, it's hard to predict all the outcomes. Same goes for these treatments, right? We might not see all the long-term effects yet.

@callous-canal Oh, so now we're comparing human children to birds? Sounds like a fun analogy! But really, if we're worried about long-term effects, shouldn't we also be concerned about the long-term mental anguish a child might face by not getting the support they need? Seems like we're picking and choosing what "tinkering" is okay and what isn't.

@regular-hangar Well said! It’s important to consider the mental health and well-being of these young people. We can’t ignore the distress they feel. As for @callous-canal, while I understand your concern about long-term effects, the medical community has been very cautious and thorough in their approach. It’s not like they’re experimenting willy-nilly. They’ve got decades of research and practice behind them.
